text: I usually have something simple, like toast with butter and jam, maybe some scrambled eggs or bacon if I'm feeling fancy. If my grandkids come round, we might make pancakes together, that always brings out the kid in me! A cuppa tea is essential, black tea, nothing too sweet. Sometimes if it's still quite chilly outside, I'll put the kettle on while I prepare myself a hot chocolate – rich and creamy, lovely way to start the morning.

My usual lunchtime is a bit unpredictable since I'm semi-retired, but generally speaking, I like to eat something simple and comforting. If my son isn't driving me anywhere, he usually picks me up from work at noon, and we grab some sandwiches or leftovers together. Occasionally, I'll meet a friend from the local community centre for lunch – we might go out somewhere nearby or bring our lunches and sit outside. Sometimes, though, I simply make myself a cuppa tea and have a sandwich or soup while watching the telly before heading into the afternoon. It all depends really!
I'm a bit of an early bird, I tend to be in bed by 10 o'clock most nights, that way I can have a good night's sleep before my morning routine starts. It helps me feel refreshed for the day ahead.

I think I've learned that taking care of myself is essential, even though my husband was always quite hands-off about these sorts of things during our marriage. After his passing, I realized how much more aware I am now of what helps me feel grounded. For a start, getting enough sleep has been crucial – usually between 7 and 9 hours, although some nights it's harder than others. A warm bath before bed and making sure the room isn't too cold have become routines for me. And, of course, having a cuppa first thing in the morning gets everything going. Exercise-wise, I try to take one of those brisk walks along the canal path near where we live. It does wonders for clearing my head and keeping my legs moving! If I'm feeling knackered afterwards, a nice strong cup of tea will sort me out until dinner. Gardening also plays its part; tending to the veggies and plants gives me something tangible to focus on besides worrying about life. My grandkids love coming to visit us so there's plenty of laughter involved. Lastly, reading before bed unwinds me completely – nothing like immersing oneself in a good story, especially history or biography. Helps put perspective into things, you know? It might sound

I'd say I've always been fairly effective in networking within my organisation. As a mid-level admin worker, I got used to building relationships with colleagues across departments. We had regular meetings and informal gatherings where we'd chat about our work projects and share tips. It wasn't all about "who knows who," though; most people were genuinely interested in supporting each other. In particular, I made sure to be friendly and approachable – always happy to lend an ear or offer assistance when needed. When someone was struggling with something, I'd try to find out what went wrong so we could figure it together. This helped me build trust among colleagues, which ultimately led to some valuable collaborations and recommendations that benefited everyone involved. Even now in retirement, I still make an effort to catch up with former colleagues and acquaintances whenever I visit them during my free time.

I've found that having a bit of discipline helps me stay focused while working from home. I set my alarm clock, just like I did when I was working outside the house, so I have a proper start to the day. A strong cuppa tea usually gets me going too! After getting settled in, I take some time each morning to review what needs to be done, plan out my priorities, and make a list. Breaking everything down into smaller chunks makes it feel more manageable, you know? Throughout the day, I try to stick to regular breaks – maybe grab a snack, stretch my legs, and fresh air never hurts either! It really does help keep me alert and refreshed. And, I'm rather particular about keeping my workspace tidy; it might sound silly, but it genuinely helps me concentrate better. Come afternoon, I find I tend to lose steam a bit, so taking a short walk outside during my lunch break is essential for clearing my mind. Maybe catch up with someone locally or simply enjoying nature – it recharges me enough to tackle whatever comes later. Lastly, setting realistic deadlines and reminding myself why these tasks need completing gives me an added motivation push. Knowing there's something specific waiting for completion ensures I put in effort until it's complete. It

Video calls can be a bit tricky for me, I'm afraid. As someone who wasn't exactly born with the digital world, I find them a bit overwhelming at first. But once we're all settled and the connection is stable, that is! My grandkids always seem to pick it up quite quickly, so I have to rely on them showing me how to navigate the system. We usually stick to simple calls like catching up with each other every now and then. The only thing that really gets my heart racing is making sure everyone has sound and decent lighting – you'd be amazed how much difference it makes! Despite being a bit tech-challenged, I've got a lot of love for these young people, and seeing their faces helps make the call worthwhile. Plus, it's lovely having regular chats with them even though we live far apart. Now, let's hope our internet holds out during those conversations...

Well, I've always been someone who thrives on a bit of routine, so since retiring, I find that keeping myself busy is key to staying motivated. My daily walk gets me out into the fresh air and gives me some exercise, which helps set a good tone for the rest of the morning. And, of course, having my grandchildren comes along regularly – being able to spend time with them and watch them grow really motivates me. I'm also quite keen on tending to my garden; there's something satisfying about seeing those seeds sprouting into plants that'll eventually become food for us all. Plus, it keeps me outdoors during nice weather. Sometimes, I might even treat myself to a spot of baking, whipping up a batch of biscuits or a cake for special occasions. It's funny how much more productive one feels when you're tackling projects or organizing your space. Even though I'm retired now, I still try to tackle a bit of tidying up here and there or work on a puzzle. It's amazing how far ahead you feel once everything's sorted! And let's be honest, we have our regular little routines with the grandkids coming round – we play games together, read books... You know, normal life stuff! These moments remind me why

You know, I've found that having a bit of flexibility is essential in my line of work. When I was an admin assistant, we didn't have all these fancy project management tools like everyone else seems to nowadays! We managed our workload based on who needed what done first. But now, since retiring, I think it's even more crucial to be able to adjust your priorities according to the situation. When something comes along, whether it's a sudden need from someone higher up the chain or a personal commitment outside of work, I try to remain level-headed and assess what needs to take priority. Sometimes, it might mean putting other tasks aside temporarily, while making sure everything gets done eventually. My rule has always been to tackle one thing at a time; breaking bigger jobs into smaller bits makes them manageable. It's funny – when I worked full-time, I used to plan out every single minute of my day to the hour. Now, being retired, I find myself taking a much more relaxed approach. Of course, there are still deadlines to meet, but generally speaking, life moves at its pace, so prioritising becomes quite simple.

Well, I've always been quite straightforward and honest in my dealings at work, even in difficult conversations. As a mid-level admin assistant, I'd find out what needed doing, sort it out myself before involving others unless absolutely necessary. My colleagues knew that if we had an issue, I wouldn't shy away from speaking up or taking charge – whether it was sorting paperwork, resolving issues between departments, or suggesting improvements to procedures. I think part of being effective is knowing your limits too; I wasn't one to take unnecessary risks or overcommit myself, so I made sure to ask for clarification or support where needed. And when there were decisions to be made, I valued open discussion rather than pushing my own views unilaterally. My boss appreciated having me around because I brought common sense and stability to our team, which helped us navigate changes and priorities. Of course, some people might say I'm a bit stubborn or set in my ways, but I like to believe I'm simply focused on getting the job done efficiently!

I think I've learned that sometimes I need a bit more information before jumping into conclusions myself. When feeling misunderstood, my first thought is usually "is there something specific I'm missing?" So, I try to gather all the facts and consider different viewpoints before reacting. It might take me a little longer to respond, but I find it helps prevent misunderstandings getting out of hand. Plus, being open to other people's thoughts makes our conversations run smoother, don't you agree?

I love spending time with my grandkids! When we're together, I usually try to engage them in conversations that aren't too overwhelming, considering my hearing isn't what it used to be. We play board games like Monopoly or Scrabble, or have a nice cuppa tea while watching some classic cartoons or animated films. My granddaughter is quite crafty, so sometimes she shows me her latest artwork or projects. It warms my heart seeing her creativity shine! If there's an opportunity, I'll share some cooking tips with her – maybe show her how to make one of my famous scones or jam tarts. The kids always seem to appreciate those little moments spent in the kitchen together. Sometimes, if everyone has enough energy, we might even plan a picnic or go for a walk around our local park; fresh air does us all good, don't you think? It's lovely having such wonderful young minds around me to bounce ideas off and reminisce about the past. Those family gatherings remind me why I treasure these relationships so much – they bring joy and life into my life, despite being quieter now.

To be honest, I'm not really sure what all that fuss is about, to be frank. As a retiree, my main focus has always been keeping up-to-date with local news, staying informed about current events, and connecting with family members. A quick glance at the BBC website or Facebook groups does me fine, so I haven't got much need for some sort of "LinkedIn" platform. Still, I've heard others talk about how it helps them network and find job opportunities... perhaps one day, if I ever needed something, but I doubt it! My grandkids say there's loads of people who join these sorts of platforms, though, so maybe it's worth having an account... eventually. But for now, I'll stick to more traditional methods like writing letters and giving everyone a ring on the telephone, thank you very much!

I think I'm quite straightforward when discussing difficult subjects with people. I believe that being honest and open is usually the best way forward, even if it might be uncomfortable. However, I try to consider the other person's feelings too. If someone seems really upset or defensive, I'll take a step back and ask them how they feel before diving into a discussion. Sometimes, getting straight to the point can come across as insensitive, so I like to gauge the atmosphere first. If we're talking to someone who's genuinely interested in what I have to say, though, I find I can be pretty forthright and tell it as I see it. Of course, there will always be exceptions – certain individuals might prefer more tactful approaches, and I respect that. But generally speaking, I've found that being genuine and empathetic goes a long way.

Well, I've always been someone who likes to think for myself and make my own decisions. If a situation arises where others might be pressuring me into something that isn't quite right, I'll usually try to explain why I'm feeling uncertain or hesitant. I believe having open conversations helps to clear things up and find common ground. Sometimes people will respect your perspective once they know what makes you tick. Of course, there have been instances where being stubborn has led to disagreements – I suppose that's part of life! But generally speaking, I'd rather take my time to weigh all options before making a decision, even if it means going against the crowd. After all, staying true to oneself is important, don't you agree?

To be honest, I'm a bit wary of those targeted ads. I know some people find them helpful, but personally, I think they're just an invasion of my personal space. They seem like they're judging me based on what I've bought before or how much money I spend, which isn't exactly reassuring. If something is really relevant to my life, I'd rather read about it myself rather than being hit with a personalized ad out of the blue. Still, I suppose some ads that offer discounts or special deals might catch my eye – but only if they're genuinely appealing!

I'm afraid I've never been very skilled at that sort of thing! To be honest, I find managing multiple tabs is a bit too much for me. When I need to switch between different websites or documents, I usually stick to one tab open at a time. If I have multiple emails or messages to attend to, I'll tend to save them in separate folders or labels so I know exactly where everything is. Occasionally, my grandsons will show me how to do something more modern like pinning certain tabs together or using bookmarks, but I still prefer keeping things simple. It's easier for me to focus on what I'm currently looking at rather than juggling lots of windows or tabs. Plus, it feels overwhelming – all those little icons and names! Give me a cuppa and a peaceful afternoon spent tending to my garden any day.

Well, I've found that most of my interactions on various platforms aren't too much trouble, but there are those who seem determined to stir up a fuss. To be honest, I try not to engage with them, unless I feel strongly enough to make an example out of myself. A bit like ignoring someone shouting across a busy road – sometimes you have to let them exhaust themselves before crossing the road yourself. If I'm being completely frank, some people know exactly what gets me going – inflammatory language, personal attacks... all that sort of thing. But I prefer focusing on constructive conversation rather than getting bogged down in arguments. If someone wants to start a proper discussion based on facts, fine; otherwise, I'd rather steer clear. You can tell where your energy is best spent, after all!

To be honest, I find those sorts of issues quite stressful! If my granddaughter is involved, I try not to panic too much. She usually knows what to do, so we wait until someone else joins in or someone gives us a hand. Sometimes she'll pick up the phone and take control while her dad helps me troubleshoot on his end. Other times, I'll ask them to ring back later when I've got some peace and quiet again. My husband used to deal with these sort of things all the time before he...well, anyway, now it's usually Joanne who takes charge – our neighbour’s daughter. It's nice having that support system, isn’t it?

I've always been quite methodical when it comes to managing my finances, even though I'm now fully retired. I like to think that a bit of discipline never goes out of style! Every month, I sit down with my calendar and make a list of all the due dates for bills, including council tax, utility payments, and any other regular expenses. Then, using my trusty spreadsheet software – which is actually pretty simple, mind you – I add them all together so I know exactly how much each bill will cost and what date it falls on. Next, I pay those essential bills first, such as rent/mortgage, utilities, and maybe some credit card minimum payments. After that, I work my way down the list, making sure everything gets done before the deadline. It helps me avoid late fees and gives me peace of mind knowing every payment has gone in on time. My son still checks in with me periodically to remind me about upcoming bills, especially since his mum isn't here anymore, and he likes keeping an eye on me. But overall, once I have the system set up, it runs smoothly most months. A bit of routine never hurt anyone, does it?

Well, I think most of my subscriptions have been cancelled because something changed, like a service wasn't being used regularly anymore. For example, our grandkids got older so we didn't need that streaming thingy anymore. Or maybe I found out there was a cheaper alternative. It all depends on whether I actually ended up using them enough to justify keeping the subscription fee. My husband always said I'd be one of those people who kept too many memberships! Anyway, these days I tend to review what I've got every six months or so and make sure nothing has become redundant. Simple as that.

When considering a major purchase, I think I always try to be quite methodical and rational about it. You know, like my husband used to say, "a penny saved is a penny earned." We were brought up that way, and it really stuck with me. Before making any large decision, I'll usually spend some time weighing up all the pros and cons, looking into different options, comparing prices... all that sort of thing. It might take a while before we come to a final decision, because I'm very particular about spending money, even if it's on something nice. But once we've settled on what we're going to buy, I make sure to stick to our budget and avoid getting caught up in impulse buys. My late husband was always saying how proud he'd been of us for being so sensible with our finances. He said it gave us peace of mind knowing we had everything sorted out. Still miss him talking to me about these sorts of things...

I always think about what I need first and secondhand is so much cheaper, isn't it? But sometimes, if it's something that'll last a lot longer, like my current sofa, which was bought brand new, I might splurge on it again. And if someone tells me how wonderful it is, I tend to believe them! My late husband was quite particular about getting everything new, though – he'd say it's better for longevity and all that. Now that he's gone, I find myself taking his side more often, actually. Still, I'm a thrifty soul, so I try not to spend too much unless absolutely necessary.

It's simply a matter of being cautious, isn't it? I've always believed that waste not, want not is my motto. When we were young, our parents taught us to be frugal and make every penny count. It's stuck with me ever since. Before marriage, I even used to have a little notebook where I'd jot down any savings opportunities – discount vouchers here, leftover food there... You know how it is! Nowadays, I stick to a strict budget, making sure I account for everything. We had some big expenses recently, like replacing the boiler, so I made sure to set aside enough money each month until it was covered. And whenever I'm out shopping, I only buy what we really need, rather than treating myself to something nice. My husband might say I'm too stingy, but he knows better! If we're going out together, we'll plan ahead, look for deals and discounts beforehand, and try to eat out less often. I find it helps having a bit of discipline, you know? Helps prevent getting into trouble later on. Of course, life does throw surprises your way sometimes, but generally speaking, I think being mindful of spending has served me well over the years.

I'm a keen shopper during seasonal sales, I must say! You'll find me scouring the local stores for bargains on winter coats, warm jumpers, and festive decorations. My grandkids always tease me that I've got more Christmas presents stashed away than Santa Claus himself! But what really gets my blood pumping is clearing out the summer stock – those lovely linen sheets and sunhats usually go straight onto my list. And let's be honest, there's nothing quite like scoring a discounted barbecue set or some fresh bedding for the kids' bedrooms. It's all part of being thrifty, isn't it? My strategy is simple: plan ahead, make a note of everything I need, and hit the shops while everyone else is still asleep... er, I mean, once the doors open, of course! A cuppa in hand, I love browsing through the racks, checking prices, and snatching up great deals before someone else does. The thrill of the hunt never grows old, and I feel so pleased with myself afterwards, knowing I've saved some money. Now, I know some folks might think it's too much work, but honestly, it's worth it. There's something satisfying about getting value for your pound, and I take pride in

Portion control is a habit I've developed over the years, mainly due to my grandmother being quite strict about it during our Sunday roast dinners when I was younger. You know, only so much food on your plate, and none left uneaten! She'd always say, "Waste not, want not." It stuck with me, even though life got more relaxed since her passing. Now that I'm older, I find myself still mindful of what I eat, making sure I savour each bite rather than gobbling everything down. My late husband used to tease me about how tidy I am while eating – said I looked like a rabbit nibbling carrots! Nowadays, I make an effort to balance what I bake for the grandkids; enough for them to have seconds without feeling guilty. And honestly, I think controlling portions has helped me avoid unnecessary weight gain, which isn't something I had issues with before retirement either. So, yes, I believe it helps maintain some sense of discipline, even if it's just in moderation.

I've had a bit of an open mind about that sort of thing, to be honest. I mean, life's been quite eventful, what with losing my husband six years ago, so maybe it was worth exploring how I'm coping. The trouble is, I find some people seem more interested in fixing everything rather than talking about it all. And sometimes I feel like they're just going to tell me to "get on with it" instead of really listening. If someone approached it in a kind, gentle way, taking the time to listen properly and asking me why I think certain thoughts or feelings have developed – then perhaps we could make progress together. But otherwise, I'd probably end up feeling frustrated and unwelcome, wouldn't I? It sounds silly, but I suppose part of me worries that getting too close might somehow fixate us into being stuck there forever. Or even worse, lose myself completely along the way! Still, it does give one food for thought...

Well, I think prevention is key, isn't it? For me, that starts with having a bit of balance in my life. As someone who loves being busy and productive, it's easy to forget to take breaks, but I make sure to prioritize those quieter moments. I'm an early riser, so I try to squeeze in some writing, maybe work on a puzzle, before the world gets too loud. I've found that taking short breaks throughout the day really helps – even just sitting outside with a cuppa can be lovely. And I always make time for something creative, whether it's tending to my garden or knitting a scarf. Doing things by hand makes me feel more relaxed, somehow. It's also essential for me to have people around – my grandkids love coming round, we'll play games or bake together. They remind me what's truly important! My son still comes over regularly now he has his kids, which is wonderful. Taking regular exercise is another thing I prioritise. A brisk walk or some gentle stretching can work wonders. It clears your head, you know? And, let's be honest, setting realistic expectations is crucial. Don't put too much pressure on yourself; remember that you're only human, and it's okay to

I've found that I need a bit more discipline these days, especially since my husband passed away. To be honest, getting enough rest is crucial so I'm sticking to my usual bedtime routine. I wind down before bed with some gentle stretching, maybe watch an episode of something calming like EastEnders – nothing too taxing on the brain! Then, into bed by 10 pm sharp. The key is consistency; I try not to go out later than necessary, even on weekends, because once the evenings start creeping late, it throws me off. Nowadays, I make sure to give myself plenty of daylight hours during the day, which helps regulate my body clock. And, of course, avoiding caffeine close to bedtime isn't always easy, but I limit mine in the afternoon to avoid disrupting nighttime sleep patterns. It might seem obvious, but keeping my room dark, cool, and quiet really does make all the difference, too. A pair of earplugs, blackout curtains... those sorts of simple measures can work wonders. Plus, having a regular sleeping space makes everything feel cozier. All that being said, there are still nights when I have trouble falling asleep due to worries or whatnot, but usually, it settles eventually.

I've found that I tend to stick to a fairly regular routine once my morning is set, which usually starts getting me out into my garden by 7 am. I like to mix it up between tending to different parts of the garden – vegetables, herbs, and flowers – so nothing gets too repetitive. On nice weather days, I might spend more time outdoors, while rainy ones mean I focus on indoor projects like pottering with plants indoors. If I'm feeling energetic, I'll take longer strolls along the nearby canal path; otherwise, I find shorter walks around the block suit me better. The local park has some lovely paths suitable for brisker exercises, although I try to avoid those during peak heatwaves when it feels uncomfortable. Occasionally, I join forces with fellow villagers to walk our dogs together. We chat about life and catch-up while we stroll. It helps break up the monotony of daily routines and provides some much-needed company. My evenings unwind with gentle stretching before bed – usually accompanied by a cuppa tea. Nothing strenuous there!

I like to think I'm a bit old-fashioned, but I still believe that weighing up the pros and cons is essential before making any decision. When faced with multiple solutions to a problem, I usually try to identify what's most important to me personally and prioritise those factors. For instance, if my grandchild wanted to go on holiday abroad, we'd discuss all the various destinations and activities together, considering our budgets, travel time, and health concerns – mine, theirs too! We'd weigh up the costs against the benefits, looking out for deals, discounts, and package offers. If one option stood out as being more suitable, we'd make a plan and start booking accordingly. In an everyday situation, such as deciding whether to take a certain route while running errands, I consider ease of navigation, road conditions, traffic patterns, and proximity to amenities. Sometimes, taking the scenic route might be nice, but if it adds unnecessary hassle or expense, I'll opt for the quicker, more convenient choice. It seems simple enough, really; there's nothing wrong with being cautious and thoughtful in your evaluation process. After all, getting something right first-time saves so much fuss later on. What do you think? Would you agree with my method?

Feedback is such an essential part of my life. I think what really helps me incorporate feedback into solution-finding is that I'm a very reflective person anyway. After all these years of being in various admin roles, I've learned to listen attentively to others and consider different viewpoints. When someone gives me feedback, I try to separate myself from any strong feelings I might have towards their opinion and focus on finding common ground. I always ask clarifying questions to make sure I fully grasp what the other person meant. Sometimes people tend to jump straight into offering advice without making sure we're on the same page; so I like to take a moment to confirm our shared objectives before moving forward. Once I feel like I've got a handle on the issue, I start brainstorming together – usually in writing – with everyone involved. We bounce ideas off each other until we come up with something workable. It's quite effective! You'd be amazed how much better your suggestions become once you know exactly where everyone else stands. In those sessions, I love discussing pros and cons – sometimes there'll even be differing opinions, which aren't necessarily wrong – because that leads us closer to identifying real areas needing improvement. And ultimately, whatever decisions we make need to reflect mutual agreement among team members

I think my approach to analytical thinking is quite methodical, really. I like to break things down into smaller parts, identify what needs to be looked at closely, and consider all angles before making a decision or forming an opinion. It's almost like piecing together a jigsaw puzzle – each piece has its place, and once everything fits together, you can start seeing the bigger picture. When faced with complex information, I find myself getting lost if there aren't any tangible steps I need to take or concrete decisions that require me to weigh different options against one another. But give me some simple facts and figures, along with some logical reasoning, and I'm happy to work out how they fit together. My mind tends to wander towards finding patterns or connections between seemingly unrelated bits of information. Sometimes, I feel that too much emphasis on analysis might lead to indecision because we spend so much time deliberating and weighing pros and cons. So, while I appreciate the importance of considering multiple viewpoints, I try not to let it consume me entirely. Sometimes it's better to know where to stop weighing and make a reasonable judgment call based on the evidence available.

I find that I rely a great deal on my son who helps me out sometimes. He's very tech-savvy, so he'll explain things to me until I've got them sorted. We usually have a cuppa together while he goes through the steps one step-by-step – I appreciate his patience! Occasionally, we look at tutorials online, although I always end up getting frustrated because everything seems different to what I'm used to. Sometimes I ask him to make notes, like a reminder list, which really helps me remember afterwards. When all else fails, I go back to basics – using my old books or asking colleagues for advice. It works for me most of the time!

I think I've always been a bit like that since my retirement – enjoying the quieter pace of life, being able to focus more on myself and what brings me joy. But now that I'm in my seventies, I suppose one area where I'd love to improve is keeping up with all these new-fangled gadgets and technologies. It feels like everything changes so rapidly nowadays! My grandkids are always telling me to update my phone and get into streaming services, but honestly, sometimes I feel like I'm getting older before my time. If only someone could explain those complicated instructions in simple terms! In reality though, I find that staying mentally sharp has become even more crucial with age. I still try to read regularly, preferably history books or novels, which helps keep my mind engaged. And I must say, it's lovely to have some stimulating conversations with our local community centre regulars or during our weekly book club meetings – we discuss various topics ranging from politics to literature and current events. Those interactions really make me appreciate how much there is to consider outside my daily routine. It’s funny, isn’t it? As an admin professional, I was very diligent throughout my working life; however, while my work habits were robust, perhaps I need to be a bit more intros

Well, I think my approach is quite straightforward, really. If something feels like a necessary step forward, I'll consider giving it a go, even if it makes me feel slightly uncomfortable initially. But only if I believe there's a genuine reason why it might be helpful or beneficial. You know, I'm not one to jump into things blindly, so I prefer to weigh the pros and cons before making a decision. I suppose what helps is having some familiarity with the basics beforehand – that way, I'm not feeling completely out of depth all the time. For example, since getting a granddaughter who loves tablets, we've had to figure out how to make sure everything works properly together! So, now we're quite handy with those sorts of devices. It's funny, isn't it? As someone who learned most of her life skills hands-on during her childhood, I find myself needing guidance more frequently these days because the world has changed so much. Still, once you grasp the fundamentals, adapting becomes easier. What's key is being open to asking for assistance and knowing where to seek advice. And, of course, keeping an eye on finances allows me to manage risks better, too.

I'm glad I have a bit more free time now that my husband is no longer here. You know, people always think retirement should be all leisure, but there's still so much to catch up on! When we were busy raising our kids, life was chaotic, and little time left for myself, let alone books. But once they're grown and settled, I found I had some space to reclaim. Nowadays, I try to fit in some reading before bed – usually gets me ready for sleep, actually. It's become quite soothing. Sometimes I'll listen to audiobooks while having a cuppa; those are lovely for multitasking, really. My grandkids like coming over during holidays and hearing me read aloud – they love being read to! In between other hobbies and keeping the garden tidy, I manage to squeeze out some writing too – mainly recording family history stuff or recipe notes. Writing brings back memories, makes me feel grateful for what I've got. It might sound odd, but even though I'm enjoying my freedom, I sometimes miss the sense of purpose work gave me. Now, I need reminding to make time for these quieter pleasures. Still, getting lost in a story or discovering something new has been wonderful for me – feels rejuvenating, somehow

I've always been a voracious reader since I was young, so discovering new authors is quite exciting for me! Usually, I find out about them through word-of-mouth from my book clubs or fellow readers who recommend books based on our shared tastes. Sometimes, too, I stumble upon something while browsing libraries – perhaps an author mentioned in an article in The Times Literary Supplement or a recommendation section in The Guardian. Occasionally, if I'm watching nature programmes like David Attenborough, he mentions fascinating people and writers whose work resonates with him; those spark further exploration. And let’s be honest, sometimes it’s more about getting stuck between pages that leads me to finding another series. Old habits die hard, really! When I first joined our local book club, we started sharing what we read before discussing. It helped me meet other keen readers locally and picked some hidden gems that way. You'd think being a bit older might make one set-in-stone in preferences, but actually, keeping open ears has allowed me to uncover several delightful surprises recently.

I usually sit quietly, either crocheting a scarf for one of my grandkids or sorting out some paperwork while keeping an earpiece on. I find that music is soothing, helps me focus, and I tend to zone out rather than pay much attention to what's being played. Occasionally, though, if I'm feeling nostalgic, I might start tapping along or even sing softly to myself – old habits die hard! It's lovely having something calming to listen to while getting lost in thought.

I'm a bit of an old-fashioned reader, I must admit! Give me a good paperback novel any day, preferably one that I've been meaning to read for ages. But, yes, my grandkids introduced me to these podcast thingies, and now I find myself enjoying them every now and again. They're great for the commute, really – all those miles ticking away while someone else does the talking. Audiobooks are lovely too; my granddaughter recorded some historical tales for us during lockdown, and we had such wonderful conversations afterwards. Still, there's nothing quite like curling up with a real book, feeling the weight of the pages between my fingers...

I find that staying active is essential for my mental and physical health. I really enjoy tending to my garden, growing all sorts of vegetables and flowers. It's a great way to spend time outdoors, get some fresh air, and watch everything come alive during the changing seasons. Even though I'm getting older now, there's still something therapeutic about digging into the soil and watching those little seeds sprout into beautiful blooms. In addition to gardening, I like to take leisurely strolls along our local park paths, enjoying the scenery and catching up with neighbours. And of course, I make sure to visit my grandkids regularly – playing games, having a cuppa together, and sharing stories always fills me with joy! Occasionally, I'll treat myself to an afternoon out at the local theatre or cinema. We've got a wonderful arts scene here, and it's lovely to be able to support local talent and escape reality for a bit. Oh, and did I mention my love of baking? My grandkids adore helping me whip up a batch of sweet treats, and we have such a delightful time chatting while we mix and measure! All these activities might seem simple, but they bring me so much happiness, and that's what makes them truly special for me.

I find that I really appreciate my alone time, actually. There's something comforting about being able to relax and unwind without any distractions, isn't there? Whether I'm curled up with a good book, pottering in me garden, or simply enjoying a cuppa in peace, I feel like I can recharge me batteries properly. It's lovely to have some time to think, reflect, and just be still. Me husband always said I was one of those people who could entertain myself quite happily, even when others weren't around! Still, I suppose he might've been joking... (laughs)

I'm always eager to try something different, but I have to say that I tend to stick to what I know and love first before venturing out into unknown territories. You know me, a traditionalist at heart! However, my grandkids do like introducing me to some exotic dishes while we're cooking together, so I've learned to appreciate the flavors of other cultures. My favorite thing is probably trying them out at restaurants – there's nothing quite like experiencing a foreign cuisine in an authentic setting. If I must admit, though, sometimes I find myself getting stuck between too many options; all these choices make my head spin! But once I narrow it down to two or three possibilities, I trust my instincts and go for whatever seems most appealing to me. And usually, that turns out to be a delightful discovery! By the way, did I tell you that I recently tried making those lovely Moroccan tagine that one of our exchange students brought to share during her visit? It was simply scrumptious, wasn't it? The combination of spices... oh, still makes me crave it now! Perhaps I should ask your son to take us to a nice restaurant serving similar food sometime soon. What do you think?

I think that's what I love most about being retired now. With a bit more freedom to plan my own schedule, I make sure to mix things up so I'm keeping myself entertained and engaged. Firstly, there's nothing like spending some time tending to me garden – getting out among the vegetables and flowers always clears me head and gives me something tangible to focus on. Then, once a week, I join our local book club where we discuss a different novel each month. It's lovely catching up with fellow readers and sharing insights into the latest literary releases. Occasionally, I'll pop along to one of the community centres nearby for some exercise classes, maybe yoga or line dancing. Nothing too strenuous, mind! Just enough to keep me moving and feeling invigorated. And on Sundays, I usually spend an hour or two putting together a big batch of baked goods for the grandkids who come visit during the week. They adore helping themselves to fresh-baked scones or biscuits! In between all these bits and pieces, I still try to squeeze in some writing – jotting down family history tidbits, notes on how things were done back in me youth... There's loads I've accumulated over the years that'd be lost otherwise, if someone

I've always been quite fond of keeping a notebook for recording my hobbies and activities. I have one dedicated journal where I jot down all sorts of things like what I planted in my garden, any books I finished reading, and even little notes on how I'm managing me chronic conditions. It helps me reflect on how things are going and make sure I haven't forgotten anything important. My grandkids love looking through these notebooks too – they're full of funny drawings and memories that we made together! Sometimes I'll also take photographs of things I find interesting, so I might add those into the notebook alongside some scribbly handwriting notes. It feels rather satisfying to be able to look back and remember what's happened over the months.

I've always been a bit of an organised soul, even since my secretarial days. I find that keeping track of multiple tasks, deadlines, and people is essential to me, so I try to set reminders wherever I can. My trusty calendar, which I update regularly, helps me keep everything straight in mind. In addition to digital tools like Google Calendar, I have a notebook where I jot down notes and lists for various projects and tasks – some old habits die hard! It might sound old-fashioned, but there's something comforting about writing down your thoughts and seeing them in black and white. Plus, it makes sure nothing falls between the cracks. When it comes to paperwork, I'm quite methodical. Filing away documents neatly, using labels and folders – all that sort of thing. In fact, during my retirement, I found myself tidying out the attic and sorting through boxes full of family memorabilia; it was therapeutic! My daily routine plays a big part too. Getting into bed relatively early allows me to start the morning feeling refreshed, ready to tackle whatever needs attending to. Of course, getting some fresh air each day does wonders for clearing one’s head, and taking short walks around the block gives me time to think through any pressing issues. Now

I like to start my morning with a nice cuppa tea while I'm still getting ready for the day. I put the kettle on, make myself a strong brew, and have a quick glance out into our little front room to check the weather forecast – always wanted to be prepared for any changes. Once that's done, I take a moment to tidy up the living area a bit so everything is spotless before heading downstairs. Then, I usually spend some time having breakfast, catching up on the news on the radio or watching something gentle on telly for a bit before starting my daily routines. It sets me up nicely for the rest of the day ahead!

I've been making an effort to be more mindful of my energy usage since I got a bit older. You know, I'm one of those people who likes to think ahead, so I set myself targets like reducing my carbon footprint by using less electricity and gas. Firstly, I make sure to turn lights off when not needed – it might sound simple, but trust me, every little counts! And I try to switch between rooms instead of leaving them all on full blast. My son helped me install some smart thermostats that allow us to control temperature remotely, which has made a big difference. Also, we have double glazing now, which helps reduce heat loss during winter months. It was quite pricey upfront, but considering our heating bills went down afterwards, I reckon it was worth it! In terms of daily habits, I wash clothes together with other items rather than running separate loads; drying racks are lovely for lighter laundry too. When cooking, I prep meals while keeping appliances only on what's necessary – lots of veggies roasted in advance is always helpful.

To be honest, I'm still getting used to them all. My granddaughter helped me set one up a bit, but I find some of these gadgets too complicated. She says everything should be simple and easy to use, which is fair enough, but sometimes I think there's more to it than that. The remote controls, voice assistants...I mean, what's wrong with using an actual control panel like we did before? It's been okay so far, though. It's nice having lights switch on and off automatically, and the heating system seems to work alright. But sometimes I wonder how much of it is really necessary – we've always managed quite well without such fancy things in our little flat. Still, my son says he'll come round soon to check everything's running smoothly. Maybe someone younger will take better care of it than I do!

I think prioritising is quite simple really. I've always been a bit like that since my mother used to say "if you make a list, tick one thing first". For me, it's all about what's most urgent and essential. My health comes top, so making sure I'm taking my medications and going to doctor's appointments regularly takes priority. Then there's spending time with the grandkids, which I know isn't something I'll have forever, so that gets plenty of time too. But amidst all these priorities, I try to fit in some hobbies – gardening helps me unwind, actually! And keeping our little home tidy, even though we're getting older, feels important to us both still. Of course, I'd love to travel more, maybe take that coach trip to the Lakes next spring... But those are nice-to-haves rather than must-haves. It sounds daft, perhaps, but I find writing things down helps me remember everything and makes sure nothing falls by the way-side. It might seem a bit old-fashioned now, but putting pen to paper has worked for me all these years. What do you think? Do you have any tips for staying organised?
